(*eesti keel* [[ˈeːsʲti ˈkeːl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Estonian "Help:IPA/Estonian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Et-eesti_keel.ogg "File:Et-eesti keel.ogg")) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_language "Finnic language") and the official language of [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"). It is written in the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") and is the first language of the majority of the country's population; it is also an official language of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). Estonian is spoken natively by about 1.1 million people: 922,000 people in Estonia and 160,000 elsewhere.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Estonian** (*eesti keel* [[ˈeːsʲti ˈkeːl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Estonian "Help:IPA/Estonian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Et-eesti_keel.ogg "File:Et-eesti keel.ogg")) is a [Finnic language](/wiki/Finnic_language "Finnic language") and the official language of [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"). It is written in the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") and is the first language of the majority of the country's population; it is also an official language of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). Estonian is spoken natively by about 1.1 million people: 922,000 people in Estonia and 160,000 elsewhere.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Estonian belongs to the [Finnic](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages") (a.k.a. Baltic Finnic) branch of the [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") (a.k.a. Uralian, or [Finno-Ugric](/wiki/Finno-Ugric_languages "Finno-Ugric languages"))[[4]](#cite_note-4) [language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family"). Other Finnic languages include [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") and several [endangered languages](/wiki/Endangered_language "Endangered language") spoken around the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and in northwestern Russia. Estonian is typically subclassified as a Southern Finnic language, and it is the second-most-spoken language among all the Finnic languages.

Alongside Finnish, [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language"), Estonian is one of the only four (out of 24) [official languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union") that are not [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_language "Indo-European language").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In terms of [linguistic morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)"), Estonian is a predominantly [agglutinative language](/wiki/Agglutinative_language "Agglutinative language"). The loss of word-final sounds is extensive, and this has made its inflectional morphology markedly more [fusional](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language"), especially with respect to noun and adjective inflection.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The transitional form from an agglutinating to a fusional language is a common feature of Estonian typologically over the course of history with the development of a rich morphological system.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Word order is considerably more flexible than in English, but the basic order is [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The speakers of the two major historical languages spoken in Estonia, North and [South Estonian](/wiki/South_Estonian "South Estonian"), are thought by some linguists to have arrived in Estonia in at least two different migration waves over two millennia ago, both groups having spoken considerably different vernacular.[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8) Some linguists have classified South Estonian as another, separate Finnic language, rather than a variety of Estonian. Modern standard Estonian evolved in the 18th and 19th centuries based on the dialects of northern Estonia.

During the Medieval and Early Modern periods, Estonian accepted many loanwords from [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"), mainly from [Middle Low German](/wiki/Middle_Low_German "Middle Low German") (Middle Saxon) and, after the 16th-century Protestant [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), from the [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") language.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/EstonianGrammar1637.jpg/250px-EstonianGrammar1637.jpg)](/wiki/File:EstonianGrammar1637.jpg)

[Estonian Grammar](/wiki/Estonian_grammar "Estonian grammar") by [Heinrich Stahl](/wiki/Heinrich_Stahl "Heinrich Stahl"), published in [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn") (Reval) in 1637

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Perno_Postimees%2C_Nr_1.png/250px-Perno_Postimees%2C_Nr_1.png)](/wiki/File:Perno_Postimees,_Nr_1.png)

In 1857, editor [Johann Voldemar Jannsen](/wiki/Johann_Voldemar_Jannsen "Johann Voldemar Jannsen") of the first Estonian-language [weekly newspaper](/wiki/Weekly_newspaper "Weekly newspaper") *[Perno Postimees](/wiki/Perno_Postimees "Perno Postimees")* welcomed readers with *"Terre, armas Eesti rahwas!"* ("Hello, dear Estonian people!")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Russa_literacy_1897.jpg/250px-Russa_literacy_1897.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russa_literacy_1897.jpg)

According to the [1897 census](/wiki/1897_census "1897 census") 96.1% of the native Estonian-speaking population (age 10 and older, roughly equally for males and females) was [literate](/wiki/Literate "Literate").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Estonian_language_in_the_Russian_Empire_%281897%29.svg/250px-Estonian_language_in_the_Russian_Empire_%281897%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Estonian_language_in_the_Russian_Empire_(1897).svg)

Geographic distribution of Estonian in the Russian Empire according to the [1897 census](/wiki/1897_census "1897 census")

Oldest written records of Estonian language date from the 13th century. The "Originates Livoniae" in the *[Livonian Chronicle of Henry](/wiki/Livonian_Chronicle_of_Henry "Livonian Chronicle of Henry")* contains Estonian place names, words and fragments of sentences.

### Estonian literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Estonian literature")]

Main article: [Estonian literature](/wiki/Estonian_literature "Estonian literature")

The earliest extant samples of connected (north) Estonian have been found in the [Kullamaa Manuscript](/wiki/Kullamaa_Manuscript "Kullamaa Manuscript") ("Kullamaa prayers") dating from 1524 and 1528.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1525, the first Estonian language book was printed. It contained a religious [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") text which, however, never reached its intended readers, as it was immediately censored and all printed copies were destroyed.

The first extant Estonian book is a bilingual German-Estonian translation of the [Lutheran catechism](/wiki/Luther%27s_Large_Catechism "Luther's Large Catechism") by S. Wanradt and J. Koell dating to 1535, during the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") period. An Estonian grammar book to be used by priests was printed in German in 1637.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") was translated into the variety of South Estonian called [Võro](/wiki/V%C3%B5ro_language "Võro language") in 1686 (northern Estonian, 1715). The two languages were united based on Northern Estonian by [Anton thor Helle](/wiki/Anton_thor_Helle "Anton thor Helle").

Writings in Estonian became more significant in the 19th century during the [Estophile Enlightenment Period](/wiki/Estophilia#Estophile_Enlightenment_Period_(1750–1840) "Estophilia") (1750–1840).

The birth of native Estonian literature was during the period 1810–1820, when the patriotic and philosophical poems by [Kristjan Jaak Peterson](/wiki/Kristjan_Jaak_Peterson "Kristjan Jaak Peterson") were published. Peterson, who was the first student to acknowledge his Estonian origin at the then German-language [University of Dorpat](/wiki/University_of_Tartu "University of Tartu"), is commonly regarded as a herald of [Estonian national literature](/wiki/Estonian_literature "Estonian literature") and considered the founder of modern Estonian poetry. His birthday, March 14, is celebrated in [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") as [Mother Tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language") Day.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A fragment from Peterson's poem "Kuu" expresses the claim reestablishing the birthright of the Estonian language:

:   *Kas siis selle maa keel*
:   *Laulutuules ei või*
:   *Taevani tõustes üles*
:   *Igavikku omale otsida?*

In English:

:   *Can the language of this land*
:   *In the wind of incantation*
:   *Rising up to the heavens*
:   *Not seek for eternity?*

    :   :   *Kristjan Jaak Peterson*

In the period from 1525 to 1917, 14,503 titles were published in Estonian; by comparison, between 1918 and 1940, 23,868 titles were published.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In modern times [A. H. Tammsaare](/wiki/A._H._Tammsaare "A. H. Tammsaare"), [Jaan Kross](/wiki/Jaan_Kross "Jaan Kross"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and [Andrus Kivirähk](/wiki/Andrus_Kivir%C3%A4hk "Andrus Kivirähk") are Estonia's best-known and most translated writers.

Estonians lead the world in book ownership, owning on average 218 books per house, and 35% of Estonians owning 350 books or more (as of 2018).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Official language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Official language")]

Writings in Estonian became significant only in the 19th century with the spread of the ideas of the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), during the [Estophile Enlightenment Period](/wiki/Estophilia#Estophile_Enlightenment_Period_(1750–1840) "Estophilia") (1750–1840). Although [Baltic Germans](/wiki/Baltic_Germans "Baltic Germans") at large regarded the future of Estonians as being a fusion with themselves, the Estophile educated class admired the ancient culture of the Estonians and their era of freedom before the conquests by Danes and Germans in the 13th century.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

When the [Republic of Estonia](/wiki/Republic_of_Estonia "Republic of Estonia") was established in 1918, Estonian became the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the newly independent country. Immediately after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in 1945, over 97% of the then population of Estonia self-identified as native ethnic Estonians[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8) and spoke the language.

When Estonia was invaded and reoccupied by the Soviet army in 1944, the status of Estonian effectively changed to one of the two official languages (Russian being the other one).[[16]](#cite_note-16) Many immigrants from Russia entered Estonia under Soviet encouragement.[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8) In the 1970s, the pressure of bilingualism for Estonians was intensified. Although teaching Estonian to non-Estonians in local schools was formally compulsory, in practice, the teaching and learning of Estonian by Russian-speakers was often considered unnecessary by the Soviet authorities.[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8)
In 1991, with the restoration of Estonia's [independence](/wiki/History_of_Estonia#Regaining_independence "History of Estonia"), Estonian went back to being the only official language in Estonia.[[17]](#cite_note-17) When Estonia joined the EU in 2004, Estonian became one of its now 24 [official languages](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_European_Union "Official languages of the European Union").

The return of former Soviet immigrants to their countries of origin at the end of the 20th century has brought the proportion of native Estonian-speakers in Estonia now back above 70%. Large parts of the first- and second-generation immigrants in Estonia have now adopted Estonian (over 50% as of the 2022 census).[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8)

An 1885 ABC-book in [Võro](/wiki/V%C3%B5ro_language "Võro language") written by Johann Hurt: "Wastne Võro keeli ABD raamat"

The Estonian dialects[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) are divided into two groups – the northern and southern dialects, historically associated with the cities of [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn") in the north and [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu "Tartu") in the south, in addition to a distinct *kirderanniku* dialect, [Northeastern coastal Estonian](/wiki/Northeastern_coastal_Estonian "Northeastern coastal Estonian").

The northern group consists of the *keskmurre* or central dialect that is also the basis for the standard language, the *läänemurre* or western dialect, roughly corresponding to [Lääne County](/wiki/L%C3%A4%C3%A4ne_County "Lääne County") and [Pärnu County](/wiki/P%C3%A4rnu_County "Pärnu County"), the *saarte murre* (islands' dialect) of [Saaremaa](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa"), [Hiiumaa](/wiki/Hiiumaa "Hiiumaa"), [Muhu](/wiki/Muhu "Muhu") and [Kihnu](/wiki/Kihnu "Kihnu"), and the *idamurre* or eastern dialect on the northwestern shore of [Lake Peipus](/wiki/Lake_Peipus "Lake Peipus").

One of the pronunciation features of the Saaremaa dialect is the lack of the 'õ' vowel. A five-metre monument erected in 2020, marking the "border" between the vowels 'õ' and 'ö', humorously makes reference to this fact.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[South Estonian](/wiki/South_Estonian "South Estonian") consists of the [Tartu](/wiki/Tartu_dialect "Tartu dialect"), [Mulgi](/wiki/Mulgi_dialect "Mulgi dialect"), [Võro](/wiki/V%C3%B5ro_language "Võro language") and [Seto](/wiki/Seto_dialect "Seto dialect") varieties. These are sometimes considered either variants of South Estonian or separate languages altogether.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Also, Seto and Võro distinguish themselves from each other less by language and more by their culture and their respective Christian confession.[[8]](#cite_note-Rannut-8)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main article: [Estonian orthography](/wiki/Estonian_orthography "Estonian orthography")

### Alphabet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Alphabet")]

Estonian employs the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") as the basis for [its alphabet](/wiki/Estonian_alphabet "Estonian alphabet"). The script adds the letters *[ä](/wiki/%C3%84 "Ä")*, *[ö](/wiki/%C3%96 "Ö")*, *[ü](/wiki/%C3%9C "Ü")*, and *[õ](/wiki/%C3%95 "Õ")*, plus the later additions *[š](/wiki/%C5%A0 "Š")* and *[ž](/wiki/%C5%BD "Ž")*. The letters *c*, *q*, *w*, *x* and *y* are limited to [proper names](/wiki/Proper_names "Proper names") of foreign origin, and *f*, *z*, *š*, and *ž* appear in loanwords and foreign names only. *Ö* and *Ü* are pronounced similarly to their equivalents in Swedish and German. Unlike in standard German but like Swedish (when followed by 'r') and Finnish, *Ä* is pronounced [æ], as in English *mat*. The vowels Ä, Ö and Ü are clearly separate [phonemes](/wiki/Phonemes "Phonemes") and inherent in Estonian, although the letter shapes come from German. The letter *[õ](/wiki/%C3%95 "Õ")* denotes /ɤ/, unrounded /o/, or a [close-mid back unrounded vowel](/wiki/Close-mid_back_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid back unrounded vowel"). It is almost identical to the [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language") [ъ](/wiki/%D0%AA "Ъ") /ɤ̞/ and the [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") [ơ](/wiki/%C6%A0 "Ơ"), and is also used to transcribe the Russian [ы](/wiki/%D0%AB "Ы").
Additionally *C*, *Q*, *W*, *X*, and *Y* are used in writing foreign [proper names](/wiki/Proper_names "Proper names"). They do not occur in [Estonian words](/wiki/Estonian_words "Estonian words"), and are not officially part of the alphabet. Including all the foreign letters, the alphabet consists of the following 32 letters:

### Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Orthography")]

Although the Estonian [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") is generally guided by phonemic principles, with each [grapheme](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme") corresponding to one [phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"), there are some historical and morphological deviations from this: for example preservation of the morpheme in [declension](/wiki/Declension "Declension") of the word (writing b, g, d in places where p, k, t is pronounced) and in the use of 'i' and 'j'.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Where it is very impractical or impossible to type *š* and *ž*, they are replaced by *sh* and *zh* in some written texts, although this is considered incorrect. Otherwise, the *h* in *sh* represents a [voiceless glottal fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative"), as in *Pasha* (*pas-ha*); this also applies to some foreign names.

Modern Estonian orthography is based on the "Newer orthography" created by [Eduard Ahrens](/wiki/Eduard_Ahrens "Eduard Ahrens") in the second half of the 19th century based on Finnish orthography. The "Older orthography" it replaced was created in the 17th century by [Bengt Gottfried Forselius](/wiki/Bengt_Gottfried_Forselius "Bengt Gottfried Forselius") and [Johann Hornung](/wiki/Johann_Hornung "Johann Hornung") based on [standard German](/wiki/German_language "German language") orthography. Earlier writing in Estonian had, by and large, used an *ad hoc* orthography based on [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Middle Low German](/wiki/Middle_Low_German "Middle Low German") orthography. Some influences of the standard German orthography – for example, writing 'W'/'w' instead of 'V'/'v' – persisted well into the 1930s.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Vowels")]

There are 9 vowels and 36 [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"), 28 of which are native to the Estonian language.[[1]](/wiki/Estonian_phonology#cite_note-1 "Estonian phonology") All nine vowels can appear as the first component of a diphthong, but only /ɑ e i o u/ occur as the second component. A vowel characteristic of Estonian is the unrounded back vowel /ɤ/, which may be [close-mid back](/wiki/Close-mid_back_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid back unrounded vowel"), [close back](/wiki/Close_back_unrounded_vowel "Close back unrounded vowel"), or [close-mid central](/wiki/Close-mid_central_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid central unrounded vowel").

Word-initial *b, d, g* occur only in loanwords and some old loanwords are spelled with *p, t, k* instead of etymological *b, d, g*: *pank* 'bank'. Word-medially and word-finally, *b, d, g* represent short plosives /p, t, k/ (may be pronounced as partially voiced consonants), *p, t, k* represent half-long plosives /pː, tː, kː/, and *pp, tt, kk* represent overlong plosives /pːː, tːː, kːː/; for example: *kabi* /kɑpi/ 'hoof' — *kapi* /kɑpːi/ 'wardrobe [gen sg] — *kappi* /kɑpːːi/ 'wardrobe [ptv sg]'.

Before and after *b, p, d, t, g, k, s, h, f, š, z, ž*, the sounds [p], [t], [k] are written as *p, t, k*, with some exceptions due to morphology or etymology.

Representation of palatalised consonants is inconsistent, and they are not always indicated.

[[ŋ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_nasal "Voiced velar nasal")] is an allophone of /n/ before /k/.

While peripheral Estonian dialects are characterized by various degrees of [vowel harmony](/wiki/Vowel_harmony "Vowel harmony"), central dialects have almost completely lost the feature. Since the standard language is based on central dialects, it has no vowel harmony either. In the standard language, the front vowels occur exclusively on the first or stressed syllable, although vowel harmony is still apparent in older texts.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Estonian grammar](/wiki/Estonian_grammar "Estonian grammar")

Typologically, Estonian represents a transitional form from an [agglutinating language](/wiki/Agglutinating_language "Agglutinating language") to a [fusional language](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language"). The canonical word order is [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") (subject–verb–object), although often debated among linguists.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In Estonian, nouns and pronouns do not have [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), but nouns and adjectives decline in fourteen cases: [nominative](/wiki/Nominative "Nominative"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive"), [partitive](/wiki/Partitive_case "Partitive case"), [illative](/wiki/Illative "Illative"), [inessive](/wiki/Inessive "Inessive"), [elative](/wiki/Elative_case "Elative case"), [allative](/wiki/Allative "Allative"), [adessive](/wiki/Adessive "Adessive"), [ablative](/wiki/Ablative "Ablative"), [translative](/wiki/Translative "Translative"), [terminative](/wiki/Terminative "Terminative"), [essive](/wiki/Essive "Essive"), [abessive](/wiki/Abessive "Abessive"), and [comitative](/wiki/Comitative "Comitative"), with the case and number of the adjective always agreeing with that of the noun (except in the terminative, essive, abessive and comitative, where there is agreement only for the number, the adjective being in the genitive form). Thus the illative for *kollane maja* ("a yellow house") is *kollasesse majja* ("into a yellow house"), but the terminative is *kollase majani* ("as far as a yellow house"). With respect to the [Proto-Finnic language](/wiki/Proto-Finnic_language "Proto-Finnic language"), elision has occurred; thus, the actual case marker may be absent, but the stem is changed, cf. *maja – majja* and the [Ostrobothnia dialect](/wiki/Swedish_dialects_in_Ostrobothnia "Swedish dialects in Ostrobothnia") of Finnish *maja – majahan*.

The verbal system has no distinct future tense[[28]](#cite_note-apj-30) (the present tense serves here) and features special forms to express an action performed by an undetermined [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") (the "impersonal").

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Main article: [Estonian vocabulary](/wiki/Estonian_vocabulary "Estonian vocabulary")

Although Estonian and the [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") have very different origins and the vocabulary is considered quite different from that of the Indo-European family,[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) one can identify many similar words in Estonian and English, for example. This is primarily because Estonian has borrowed nearly one-third of its vocabulary from Germanic languages, mainly from Low Saxon ([Middle Low German](/wiki/Middle_Low_German "Middle Low German")) during the period of [German rule](/wiki/History_of_Estonia#Estonian_Crusade:_The_Middle_Ages "History of Estonia"), and [High German](/wiki/High_German "High German") (including [standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German")). The percentage of Low Saxon and High German loanwords can be estimated at 22–25 percent, with Low Saxon making up about 15 percent.[[29]](#cite_note-31) Prior to the wave of new loanwords from English in the 20th and 21st centuries, historically, Swedish and Russian were also sources of borrowings but to a much lesser extent.[[30]](#cite_note-32) In borrowings, often 'b' and 'p' are interchangeable, for example 'baggage' becomes 'pagas', 'lob' (to throw) becomes 'loopima'. The initial letter 's' before another consonant is often dropped, for example 'skool' becomes 'kool', 'stool' becomes 'tool'.

### *Ex nihilo* lexical enrichment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Ex nihilo lexical enrichment")]

Estonian [language planners](/wiki/Language_planner "Language planner") such as [Ado Grenzstein](/wiki/Ado_Grenzstein "Ado Grenzstein") (a journalist active in Estonia from the 1870s to the 1890s) tried to use formation *[ex nihilo](/wiki/Estonian_vocabulary#Ex_nihilo_lexical_enrichment "Estonian vocabulary")* (*Urschöpfung*);[[31]](#cite_note-Zuckermann_2003_p149-33) i.e. they created new words out of nothing.

The best-known reformer of Estonian, [Johannes Aavik](/wiki/Johannes_Aavik "Johannes Aavik") (1880–1973), used creations *ex nihilo* (cf. 'free constructions', Tauli 1977), along with other sources of lexical enrichment such as derivations, compositions and loanwords (often from Finnish; cf. [Saareste](/wiki/Andrus_Saareste "Andrus Saareste") and Raun 1965: 76). In Aavik's dictionary (1921) lists approximately 4000 words. About 40 of the 200 words created by Johannes Aavik allegedly *ex nihilo* are in common use today. Examples are *ese* 'object', *kolp* 'skull', *liibuma* 'to cling', *naasma* 'to return, come back', *nõme* 'stupid, dull'.[[31]](#cite_note-Zuckermann_2003_p149-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)

Many of the coinages that have been considered (often by Aavik himself) as words concocted *ex nihilo* could well have been influenced by foreign lexical items; for example, words from [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"), [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"). Aavik had a broad classical education and knew [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"). Consider *roim* 'crime' versus [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") *crime* or *taunima* 'to condemn, disapprove' versus [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") *tuomita* 'to condemn, to judge' (these Aavikisms appear in Aavik's 1921 dictionary). These words might be better regarded as a peculiar manifestation of morpho-phonemic adaptation of a foreign lexical item.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

## Example text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Estonian_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Example text")]

Article 1 of the *[Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights")* in Estonian and English:

:   *Kõik inimesed sünnivad vabadena ja võrdsetena oma väärikuselt ja õigustelt. Neile on antud mõistus ja südametunnistus ja nende suhtumist üksteisesse peab kandma vendluse vaim.*[[34]](#cite_note-36)

:   *All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.*[[35]](#cite_note-37)
